questions.question: Who invented the telephone, receiving the first patent in 1876?
questions.explanation: Alexander Graham Bell received US Patent 174,465 for the telephone on March 7, 1876 — widely regarded as the most valuable patent ever issued. The first successful voice transmission was made three days later. Bell's achievement is disputed — Elisha Gray filed a patent caveat the same day, and Italian inventor Antonio Meucci had developed a voice communication device earlier but couldn't afford the patent fees.
questions.memory_hook: Bell = telephone patent, 1876. Most valuable patent in history. He filed hours before a competitor. He went on to help found AT&T. Ironically, he refused to have a phone in his study — he found it disruptive.

questions.question: Which Roman emperor, who reigned from 27 BC to 14 AD, is considered the first true Roman Emperor and gave his name to the month of August?
questions.explanation: Augustus (born Gaius Octavius) became Rome's first emperor after defeating Mark Antony and Cleopatra in 31 BC. He transformed Rome from a republic into an empire and presided over the 'Pax Romana' — two centuries of relative peace. The month of August was renamed in his honor.
questions.memory_hook: Augustus = first Roman Emperor. August named after him. Pax Romana.

questions.question: The Srebrenica massacre in 1995, in which over 8,000 Bosnian Muslims were killed, occurred during which conflict?
questions.explanation: The Srebrenica massacre occurred during the Bosnian War (1992-1995) when Bosnian Serb forces under General Ratko Mladic killed over 8,000 Bosniak Muslim men and boys in and around Srebrenica, a UN-designated safe area. It was the worst atrocity in Europe since World War II. The International Criminal Tribunal for the Former Yugoslavia ruled it genocide. Mladic was sentenced to life imprisonment in 2017.
questions.memory_hook: Srebrenica = 8,000 murdered, 1995 Bosnia. The worst European atrocity since WWII. A UN 'safe zone' with Dutch peacekeepers who stood down. It happened while the world watched. The Hague convicted Mladic of genocide in 2017 — 22 years later.

questions.question: In ice hockey, how many players per team are on the ice during regulation play (not including the goaltender)?
questions.explanation: Each ice hockey team has 5 skaters (forwards and defensemen) plus a goaltender on the ice for a total of 6 players per team during regulation. When a team takes a penalty, one of their skaters must leave the ice, giving the opposing team a 'power play' with a 6-on-5 advantage. Teams may also pull their goaltender for an extra skater in desperate situations.
questions.memory_hook: Hockey = 5 skaters + 1 goalie = 6 per team. Take a penalty: one player goes in the box, team plays 4 skaters (5-on-4 power play). Pull the goalie: 6 skaters but no one guarding the net. Hockey has more tactical situations than most sports because of these fluid numbers.

questions.question: Who became the first Black man to win the Wimbledon men's singles title, achieving the feat in 1975 and also co-founding the National Junior Tennis League to increase access to the sport?
questions.explanation: Arthur Ashe won Wimbledon in 1975, defeating Jimmy Connors in a tactical masterclass. He was the first Black player to win the US Open (1968), Australian Open (1970), and Wimbledon (1975). He later contracted HIV from a blood transfusion and became an activist before his death in 1993.
questions.memory_hook: Arthur Ashe: first Black Wimbledon champion, 1975. Defeated Connors.

questions.question: Which legendary rock guitarist is known as 'Slowhand' and recorded 'Layla' and 'Tears in Heaven'?
questions.explanation: Eric Clapton earned the nickname 'Slowhand' from his habit of restringing guitars mid-concert while the audience clapped. He is one of rock's greatest guitarists, with a career spanning six decades. 'Layla' (1970, with Derek and the Dominos) is widely considered one of the greatest rock songs. 'Tears in Heaven' (1992) was written after the death of his four-year-old son Conor. Clapton is the only musician inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame three times.
questions.memory_hook: Eric Clapton = 'Slowhand.' 'Layla' is about his obsession with George Harrison's wife Pattie Boyd — he later married her. 'Tears in Heaven' was written after his son fell out of a New York apartment window. Three times in the Rock Hall of Fame.

questions.question: Which 1986 Oliver Stone film about the Vietnam War starred Tom Berenger and Willem Dafoe?
options: Born on the Fourth of July
questions.explanation: Platoon (1986), written and directed by Oliver Stone based on his own Vietnam War experiences, won four Academy Awards including Best Picture and Best Director. It starred Tom Berenger as the hardened Sergeant Barnes and Willem Dafoe as the moral Sergeant Elias, representing the war's duality. It was the first major Hollywood film to portray the Vietnam War from an enlisted soldier's perspective.
questions.memory_hook: Platoon = Oliver Stone's personal Vietnam War film. He served in Vietnam and wrote it from experience. Tom Berenger vs Willem Dafoe = the war's moral duality. Won Best Picture 1986. The helicopter-lifting image of Dafoe's arms raised in the air became one of cinema's most iconic images.

questions.question: Which artist created the iconic 'Thriller' music video, often called the greatest music video ever made?
questions.explanation: The 'Thriller' music video (1983) was directed by John Landis, who also directed An American Werewolf in London and Animal House. The 14-minute short film cost $500,000 (revolutionary for the time), featured Michael Jackson transforming into a zombie, and was the first music video to be added to the National Film Registry by the Library of Congress. It transformed music videos from promotional tools into cinematic art forms.
questions.memory_hook: 'Thriller' video directed by John Landis — who also made An American Werewolf in London. Perfect choice. $500,000 budget in 1983. 14 minutes. The first music video in the Library of Congress. Jackson insisted on the disclaimer at the start because his Jehovah's Witness faith had issues with occult imagery.

questions.question: Which African country is home to Victoria Falls, one of the world's largest and most spectacular waterfalls?
questions.explanation: Victoria Falls sits on the Zambezi River on the border between Zimbabwe and Zambia. The falls are approximately 1,708 meters wide and 108 meters high — making them the world's largest sheet of falling water by area. The local name is 'Mosi-oa-Tunya' meaning 'The Smoke That Thunders.' David Livingstone was the first European to see them in 1855 and named them after Queen Victoria.
questions.memory_hook: Victoria Falls = Zimbabwe AND Zambia share it. 'Mosi-oa-Tunya' — The Smoke That Thunders. The spray can be seen 50 km away. Livingstone named it Victoria to honor the queen. The local name is better.

questions.question: What is the study of heredity and genetic variation in living organisms?
questions.explanation: Genetics is the branch of biology that studies heredity — how traits are passed from parents to offspring — and genetic variation. Gregor Mendel, an Austrian monk, laid the foundation of genetics in the 1860s with his pea plant experiments. The discovery of DNA's structure (1953) transformed genetics from observation to molecular understanding. Today, genetic science underpins medicine, agriculture, forensics, and evolutionary biology.
questions.memory_hook: Genetics = study of heredity. Mendel and his pea plants in the 1860s. Watson and Crick's DNA structure in 1953. CRISPR gene editing in 2012. Genetics went from 'I wonder why some peas are wrinkled' to editing the code of life in 150 years.

questions.question: The Everglades, a unique subtropical wetland covering about 6,000 km², is located at the southern tip of which U.S. state?
questions.explanation: The Everglades is a subtropical wetland ecosystem covering southern Florida, often described as a 'river of grass' — a slow-moving sheet of water flowing from Lake Okeechobee south to Florida Bay. It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and home to alligators, American crocodiles, Florida panthers, manatees, and vast numbers of wading birds. The Everglades has been severely reduced by drainage for agriculture and urban development, losing over 50% of its original area. Restoration efforts are ongoing.
questions.memory_hook: Everglades: subtropical wetland, southern Florida. 'River of grass' — a slow-moving sheet of water, not a swamp. UNESCO World Heritage Site. Home to alligators AND crocodiles (one of the few places in the US where both live). Has lost 50%+ of its original area to drainage. The largest subtropical wilderness in the US.

questions.question: RNA serves as a messenger between DNA and protein production. What are the three main types of RNA and their roles?
options: Ribosomal, transfer, and messenger RNA each play distinct roles in protein synthesis
questions.answer: Ribosomal, transfer, and messenger RNA each play distinct roles in protein synthesis
questions.explanation: The three main types of RNA are: mRNA (messenger RNA) — copies genetic instructions from DNA and carries them to ribosomes; rRNA (ribosomal RNA) — forms the structural and catalytic core of ribosomes where protein synthesis occurs; and tRNA (transfer RNA) — brings the correct amino acid to the ribosome, matching each codon in mRNA. Together they translate genetic code into proteins. mRNA vaccines (like COVID vaccines) deliver mRNA instructions for cells to produce a specific protein.
questions.memory_hook: mRNA: carries DNA's instructions to ribosomes. rRNA: forms the ribosome itself. tRNA: delivers amino acids matching each codon. Together: DNA → mRNA → ribosome reads mRNA → tRNA brings amino acids → protein forms. mRNA vaccines hijack this process to train your immune system.
